disclaimer: ALL content in this file is generated by AI for audit preparation assistance only. It has NO regulatory authority. It must NEVER be presented to auditors as regulatory text or official ECC requirements. Official compliance determinations must reference Layer 1 verbatim text from ECC-2:2024.

audit_questions: Are cybersecurity requirements for third-party contracts and agreements formally identified, documented, and approved?
Do the requirements cover all categories of third-party relationships (IT outsourcing, cybersecurity outsourcing, managed services, SaaS vendors, professional services)?
Is the document approved by the Authorized Official?
Is there a process to identify all third-party relationships that are in scope for these requirements?
suggested_evidence: Third-party cybersecurity requirements document with Authorized Official approval
Third-party inventory showing all in-scope vendor relationships
Scope definition covering IT outsourcing, cybersecurity outsourcing, and managed services
indicators_of_implementation: Approved requirements document exists and covers all third-party categories
A third-party inventory is maintained to ensure all relationships are in scope
Requirements are communicated to procurement and legal teams

maturity_signals.initial: No formal third-party cybersecurity requirements; vendor contracts have no cybersecurity clauses
maturity_signals.developing: Requirements documented for some vendor categories but not all; not formally approved
maturity_signals.defined: Approved, comprehensive requirements covering all third-party categories; inventory maintained
maturity_signals.advanced: Third-party requirements aligned with supply chain security frameworks; tiered requirements based on vendor risk classification

possible_documents: Procurement and Legal Guidance on Cybersecurity Clauses

audit_questions: Do third-party contracts and SLAs include all three minimum cybersecurity requirements: NDA/data removal (4-1-2-1), incident communication procedures (4-1-2-2), and obligation to comply with entity's cybersecurity requirements (4-1-2-3)?
Is there a standard contract clause library ensuring all three requirements are present in every qualifying agreement?
Are contracts reviewed by legal and cybersecurity teams before signing to verify clause inclusion?
Are existing contracts reviewed and updated to include missing clauses?
suggested_evidence: Standard contract clause library containing all three minimum cybersecurity requirements
Sample signed third-party contracts showing all three clauses are present
Contract review checklist used by legal and cybersecurity teams
Remediation records for legacy contracts that lacked required clauses
indicators_of_implementation: A standard clause library is used for all qualifying contracts
All three minimum requirements are present in all active qualifying contracts
Contract review process ensures cybersecurity clause completeness before signing

maturity_signals.initial: Third-party contracts have no cybersecurity clauses
maturity_signals.developing: Some clauses exist in some contracts; coverage is inconsistent; no standard library
maturity_signals.defined: All three clauses in all qualifying contracts; standard library used; legal/cyber review before signing
maturity_signals.advanced: Automated contract review tool checks for clause completeness; clause library reviewed annually; contracts with critical vendors include right-to-audit clauses

possible_documents: Sample Third-Party Contracts with Required Clauses
possible_technical_evidence: Contract management system showing clause compliance status per vendor
Automated contract review tool output

audit_questions: Do all qualifying third-party contracts include non-disclosure clauses?
Do contracts include a clause requiring the third party to securely remove/return the entity's data upon service termination?
Does the NDA cover data learned or processed during and after the contract period?
Are data removal/return procedures defined — specifying timeline, method, and certification of destruction?
suggested_evidence: Contract clause template showing NDA and data removal/return obligations
Sample contracts confirming both clauses are present
Data return/destruction certificates received from terminated third parties
Offboarding procedure for third-party data requiring secure removal or return
indicators_of_implementation: All qualifying contracts contain both an NDA clause and a data removal/return clause
The data removal clause specifies timeline, method (return or certified destruction), and requires a certificate of completion
Data removal/return is tracked and certified for terminated vendor relationships

maturity_signals.initial: No NDA or data removal clauses in third-party contracts
maturity_signals.developing: NDA present in some contracts; data removal clause absent or unenforceable
maturity_signals.defined: Both clauses in all qualifying contracts; data removal tracked and certified at termination
maturity_signals.advanced: Data removal timelines tracked in vendor management system; certificates of destruction archived; legal review triggers on contract expiry

possible_documents: Contract NDA and Data Removal Clause Template
Data Return / Destruction Certificates from Third Parties
possible_technical_evidence: Vendor management system showing contract clause status
Data destruction certificates archive

audit_questions: Do qualifying third-party contracts include defined procedures for communication in the event of a cybersecurity incident involving the third party?
Does the clause specify: who is notified, within what timeframe, through which channel, and what information must be included?
Are notification timelines aligned with the entity's own incident response SLAs and NCA reporting requirements?
Is there evidence that these procedures are tested or exercised with key vendors?
suggested_evidence: Contract clause defining incident communication procedures (named contacts, timelines, channels, required notification content)
Sample vendor contracts showing the incident communication clause
Records of incident notifications received from third parties or sent to third parties
Tabletop exercise records testing third-party incident communication
indicators_of_implementation: All qualifying contracts include an incident communication clause with defined timelines (e.g., notify within 24 hours of discovery)
The clause specifies contact information, communication channel, and required incident details
Procedures are exercised at least annually with critical vendors

maturity_signals.initial: No incident communication procedures in third-party contracts
maturity_signals.developing: Generic incident notification clause exists; timelines and contacts undefined
maturity_signals.defined: Defined timelines, contacts, and content requirements; exercised with critical vendors
maturity_signals.advanced: Automated incident notification workflows integrated with vendor portal; notification SLAs monitored; NCA reporting obligations reflected in vendor notification timelines

possible_technical_evidence: Vendor management portal incident reporting workflow
Incident notification tracking records

audit_questions: Do qualifying third-party contracts include a clause obligating the third party to comply with the entity's cybersecurity requirements and policies?
Does the clause also require compliance with relevant legislative and regulatory requirements?
Is there a mechanism to verify third-party compliance with the entity's requirements (audits, assessments, attestations)?
Are non-compliant third parties held accountable under the contract?
suggested_evidence: Contract clause obligating third-party compliance with entity's cybersecurity policies and applicable regulations
Third-party compliance assessment or security questionnaire records
Right-to-audit clause in contracts with critical vendors
Records of compliance follow-up or remediation for non-compliant vendors
indicators_of_implementation: All qualifying contracts include the compliance obligation clause
Compliance with the entity's requirements is verified — not just contractually required
Non-compliant vendors are required to remediate, and unresolved non-compliance is escalated

maturity_signals.initial: No compliance obligation in third-party contracts; entity has no contractual basis to require compliance
maturity_signals.developing: Compliance clause exists but is never verified; no right-to-audit
maturity_signals.defined: Compliance clause in all qualifying contracts; periodic compliance verification via questionnaire or audit; non-compliance followed up
maturity_signals.advanced: Risk-tiered verification: critical vendors audited on-site; others via attestation; compliance status tracked in vendor risk register; non-compliance triggers contract penalty or termination

possible_technical_evidence: Vendor risk management platform compliance scoring
Completed vendor security questionnaires
Audit right exercise records

audit_questions: Do contracts with IT outsourcing, cybersecurity outsourcing, and managed service providers include all minimum requirements: pre-contract risk assessment (4-1-3-1) and KSA location requirement for remote-access managed service centers (4-1-3-2)?
Is there a defined process to identify which third-party contracts fall into this higher-risk category?
Is the scope of 'IT outsourcing, cybersecurity outsourcing, and managed services' clearly defined so no qualifying relationship is missed?
Are additional cybersecurity requirements (beyond the two minimum) included for the highest-risk outsourcing relationships?
suggested_evidence: Contract requirements document for IT/cybersecurity outsourcing and managed services
Inventory of all IT outsourcing, cybersecurity outsourcing, and managed service relationships
Evidence that both minimum requirements are present in all qualifying contracts
Risk-tiered additional requirements for highest-risk outsourcing relationships
indicators_of_implementation: Qualifying relationships are identified and an inventory is maintained
Both minimum requirements (pre-contract risk assessment and KSA location for remote-access managed service centers) are applied
Contracts include requirements beyond the two minimums for critical outsourcing relationships

maturity_signals.initial: No differentiated requirements for managed services or outsourcing; treated same as ordinary vendors
maturity_signals.developing: Some additional requirements for managed services; coverage of both minimums is inconsistent
maturity_signals.defined: Both minimum requirements applied to all qualifying relationships; qualifying relationships inventoried
maturity_signals.advanced: Tiered requirements based on risk level; continuous monitoring of managed service provider security posture; right-to-audit exercised for critical providers

possible_documents: IT/Cybersecurity Outsourcing and Managed Services Contract Requirements
possible_technical_evidence: Vendor risk management platform records for managed service providers

audit_questions: Is a cybersecurity risk assessment conducted before signing contracts with IT outsourcing, cybersecurity outsourcing, or managed service providers?
Is a risk assessment also required when there are changes to relevant legislative or regulatory requirements affecting existing contracts?
Do risk assessments confirm that risk mitigation controls are in place before the contract is signed?
Are risk assessment results documented and used as a basis for contractual security requirements?
suggested_evidence: Pre-contract cybersecurity risk assessment reports for all IT outsourcing and managed service agreements
Risk assessment methodology for third-party outsourcing engagements
Evidence that risk assessments triggered contract amendments when regulatory requirements changed
Contractual security requirements derived from pre-contract risk assessment findings
indicators_of_implementation: Pre-contract risk assessment is a mandatory gate before signing any IT outsourcing or managed service contract
Risk assessment covers the third party's security posture, access to entity assets, and regulatory compliance
Contracts are not signed until risk mitigation controls are confirmed as in place
Regulatory changes trigger re-assessment of existing managed service contracts

maturity_signals.initial: No pre-contract risk assessment for IT outsourcing or managed services
maturity_signals.developing: Risk assessments conducted occasionally; not a mandatory gate; findings not linked to contracts
maturity_signals.defined: Mandatory pre-contract risk assessment; risk mitigation confirmed before signing; regulatory changes trigger re-assessment
maturity_signals.advanced: Continuous third-party risk monitoring; pre-contract assessment supplemented by ongoing security rating service (BitSight, SecurityScorecard); automated contract amendment triggers on risk rating change

possible_documents: Contract Sign-Off Gate Checklist (requiring risk assessment completion)
possible_technical_evidence: Risk assessment tool reports for managed service providers
Vendor risk management platform records showing pre-contract assessment completion
Security rating service reports (BitSight, SecurityScorecard)

audit_questions: Are all cybersecurity managed service centers that use remote access to the entity's environment physically located in the Kingdom of Saudi Arabia?
Is the KSA location requirement contractually documented for all qualifying managed service providers?
Is there a process to verify that managed service centers are actually located in KSA — not just contractually required to be?
Are any exceptions to this requirement documented, justified, and approved?
suggested_evidence: Contractual clause requiring KSA location for all cybersecurity managed service centers using remote access
Documentation of physical location verification for all qualifying managed service centers (visit records, legal entity registration in KSA, datacenter address)
List of all cybersecurity managed service providers with remote access and their confirmed KSA location
Exception records for any providers not fully KSA-located, with formal approval
indicators_of_implementation: All contracts with cybersecurity managed service providers include the KSA location requirement
Physical location of qualifying managed service centers has been verified — contractual attestation is supplemented by evidence
No qualifying managed service center is operating from outside KSA without formal exception approval

maturity_signals.initial: No KSA location requirement in contracts; managed service centers may be outside KSA
maturity_signals.developing: KSA requirement in contracts; location not independently verified
maturity_signals.defined: KSA location contractually required and verified for all qualifying providers; exceptions formally approved
maturity_signals.advanced: Periodic re-verification of KSA location; clause triggers contract termination for breach; NCA notified of non-compliant providers per applicable requirements

possible_documents: Managed Service Provider Contract (KSA location clause)
KSA Location Verification Records per Managed Service Provider
possible_technical_evidence: Managed service provider legal entity registration evidence in KSA
Remote access connection source IP verification records (confirming KSA-originating connections)
Data center location certificates

audit_questions: Are third-party cybersecurity requirements reviewed on a defined periodic schedule?
Are reviews triggered by: new regulatory requirements, significant vendor incidents, changes to the third-party relationship, or material changes to the vendor's security posture?
Are changes to requirements reflected in contract amendments and communicated to procurement and legal?
Is the third-party inventory reviewed at the same time to ensure all new relationships are captured?
suggested_evidence: Third-party cybersecurity requirements review log
Version history of third-party requirements document
Records of requirement updates triggered by vendor incidents or regulatory changes
Contract amendment records reflecting updated requirements
indicators_of_implementation: Review cycle is defined and documented
Most recent review falls within the cycle
Updates are reflected in the contract clause library and communicated to procurement and legal

maturity_signals.initial: Requirements never reviewed; clause library is stale
maturity_signals.developing: Occasional reviews with no defined schedule; not triggered by incidents
maturity_signals.defined: Periodic review; triggered by regulatory changes and vendor incidents; updates to clause library communicated
maturity_signals.advanced: Review integrated with enterprise risk management cycle; vendor incidents trigger immediate requirement review; NCA regulatory updates are monitored and acted upon within a defined SLA

audit_questions: Are cybersecurity requirements for cloud computing and hosting services formally identified, documented, and approved?
Do requirements cover all cloud service models (IaaS, PaaS, SaaS) and deployment models (public, private, hybrid) used by the entity?
Is the document approved by the Authorized Official?
Is there an inventory of all cloud and hosting services used by the entity to ensure full scope coverage?
suggested_evidence: Cloud computing and hosting cybersecurity requirements document with Authorized Official approval
Cloud / hosting service inventory showing all in-scope services and their models
Scope statement covering IaaS, PaaS, and SaaS services
indicators_of_implementation: Approved requirements document exists covering all cloud and hosting service types
A cloud service inventory ensures no cloud service is out of scope
Shadow IT discovery process identifies unauthorized cloud services

maturity_signals.initial: No cloud security requirements; cloud services adopted without cybersecurity governance
maturity_signals.developing: Requirements documented for some cloud services; IaaS covered but SaaS and PaaS excluded
maturity_signals.defined: Approved requirements covering all cloud service and deployment models; inventory maintained
maturity_signals.advanced: Cloud security requirements aligned with CSA CCM; Cloud Access Security Broker (CASB) enforces requirements; shadow IT discovery active

possible_technical_evidence: CASB inventory of cloud services in use
Cloud service management platform inventory export

audit_questions: Are cloud computing and hosting cybersecurity requirements implemented across all in-scope cloud services?
Are all applicable controls from Domains 1, 2, and 3 and Subdomain 4-1 applied to cloud environments?
Are cloud-specific security configurations and baselines enforced?
Is cloud security compliance monitored continuously?
suggested_evidence: Cloud security baseline configuration per cloud service (e.g., CIS Benchmark for AWS/Azure/GCP applied)
Cloud compliance monitoring reports (native tools or third-party)
Evidence that Domain 1-3 controls are applied to cloud assets (asset inventory, IAM, logging, vulnerability management, backup, etc.)
Cloud security posture assessment results
indicators_of_implementation: Cloud security baselines are applied and compliance is monitored
IAM, logging, patching, vulnerability management, and backup controls are implemented for cloud assets
Cloud security posture is assessed and findings are remediated

maturity_signals.initial: Cloud services deployed with default provider configurations; no entity security controls applied
maturity_signals.developing: Some controls applied to IaaS; SaaS and PaaS configurations not managed
maturity_signals.defined: Security baselines applied to all cloud services; compliance monitored; all domain controls applied to cloud
maturity_signals.advanced: Cloud Security Posture Management (CSPM) with continuous compliance; Infrastructure-as-Code security scanning; CASB for SaaS governance; automated remediation of misconfigurations

possible_technical_evidence: AWS Security Hub / Azure Security Center / GCP Security Command Center compliance reports
CSPM tool output (Prisma Cloud, Wiz, Orca Security)
CIS Benchmark compliance scan results for cloud accounts
Cloud IAM configuration export

audit_questions: Do cloud and hosting service agreements include both minimum requirements: data protection per classification with data return obligation (4-2-3-1) and environment separation (4-2-3-2)?
Are applicable controls from Domains 1-3 and Subdomain 4-1 also applied in addition to the two minimum cloud-specific requirements?
Are cloud providers' commitments for both requirements verified — not just contractually stated?
Note: The previous ECC-1 requirement for KSA data localization has been removed in ECC-2; data localization is now governed by NDMO/Saudi Data and AI Authority requirements.
suggested_evidence: Cloud service agreements containing both minimum cybersecurity requirements
Data protection verification evidence from cloud providers (SOC 2, ISO 27001, CSA STAR certifications)
Environment separation verification evidence (dedicated tenancy configuration, network isolation certificates)
Reference to applicable Domain 1-3 controls applied in the cloud environment
indicators_of_implementation: Both minimum requirements are present in all cloud and hosting service contracts
Cloud provider certifications or technical evidence verify each requirement — not just contractual assertion
Applicable controls from other domains are explicitly applied to the cloud environment

maturity_signals.initial: Neither minimum requirement addressed in cloud contracts or implementation
maturity_signals.developing: One of two requirements addressed; no independent verification of provider claims
maturity_signals.defined: Both requirements in contracts; provider certifications verify data protection and environment separation
maturity_signals.advanced: Continuous verification via CSPM; cloud provider audit reports reviewed annually; shared responsibility model documented per cloud service; multi-cloud security strategy

possible_documents: Cloud Service Agreement (showing both minimum requirements)
Cloud Provider Compliance Certifications (SOC 2, ISO 27001, CSA STAR)
possible_technical_evidence: Cloud provider dedicated tenancy configuration
Network isolation configuration between tenant environments
Cloud provider security audit reports

audit_questions: Do cloud and hosting agreements require the provider to protect the entity's data in accordance with its classification level?
Is the entity's data classification scheme communicated to cloud providers with corresponding protection requirements per tier?
Does the contract require the provider to return the entity's data in a usable format upon service termination?
Has data return been verified in practice — is the data actually retrievable in a usable format, or is there risk of lock-in?
suggested_evidence: Cloud contract clause requiring data protection per entity classification level
Data classification requirements communicated to cloud providers
Contract clause specifying data return timeline, format, and completeness upon service end
Data portability test or exit strategy documentation confirming data is retrievable in a usable format
Cloud provider data protection certifications relevant to entity data classification levels
indicators_of_implementation: Cloud contracts require data protection aligned with the entity's classification scheme
Data return obligations are defined — including timeline, format (non-proprietary where possible), and completeness
The entity has tested data portability or has a documented exit strategy confirming data retrievability

maturity_signals.initial: No data protection or return clauses in cloud contracts; data may be inaccessible upon termination
maturity_signals.developing: Data protection clause exists but not classification-aligned; return clause absent or vague
maturity_signals.defined: Classification-aligned data protection required; return in usable format contractually guaranteed; exit strategy documented
maturity_signals.advanced: Annual data portability test confirms retrieval in usable format; data lock-in risk assessed for each cloud service; encryption keys held by entity (BYOK) for highest-classification data

possible_documents: Cloud Contract Data Protection and Return Clauses
Data Classification Requirements Communicated to Providers
Cloud Exit Strategy / Data Portability Plan
possible_technical_evidence: Data export test records showing retrieval in usable format
BYOK (Bring Your Own Key) configuration for highest-classification data
Cloud provider data export API documentation and test results

audit_questions: Do cloud and hosting agreements require logical separation of the entity's environment from other tenants?
Does the separation requirement explicitly cover virtual servers and cloud compute resources?
Is multi-tenancy isolation independently verified — beyond contractual assurance alone?
Are dedicated or isolated environments used for the entity's highest-classification workloads?
Is there monitoring in place to detect unauthorized cross-tenant access?
suggested_evidence: Cloud contract clause requiring tenant environment separation and virtual server isolation
Cloud provider isolation certifications or technical documentation (hypervisor isolation, network segmentation between tenants)
Configuration showing VPC/VNet/project-level isolation of entity resources
Dedicated instance or bare-metal configuration for highest-risk workloads
Cloud security assessment confirming tenant isolation controls are effective
indicators_of_implementation: Tenant isolation is contractually required and technically verified
Entity cloud resources are in dedicated VPCs/VNets/projects with no shared paths to other tenants
Highest-classification workloads use dedicated compute resources where applicable

maturity_signals.initial: No isolation requirements; entity workloads on default shared multi-tenant infrastructure with no additional controls
maturity_signals.developing: Contractual isolation requirement exists; not independently verified; all workloads on shared infrastructure
maturity_signals.defined: Contractual requirement and technical verification; VPC/VNet isolation; highest-risk workloads on dedicated infrastructure
maturity_signals.advanced: Dedicated cloud environments for Tier 1 workloads; confidential computing (TEE) for most sensitive data; CSPM monitoring for cross-tenant exposure; annual cloud architecture security review

possible_documents: Cloud Provider Tenant Isolation Technical Documentation
possible_technical_evidence: VPC/VNet configuration showing entity isolation
Dedicated instance configuration for sensitive workloads
Cloud provider hypervisor isolation certification
CSPM alert configuration for cross-tenant exposure risks
Network Access Control Lists (NACLs) and Security Group configuration

audit_questions: Are cloud computing and hosting cybersecurity requirements reviewed on a defined periodic schedule?
Are reviews triggered by: new cloud services adoption, changes to cloud provider security posture, regulatory updates (NCA, NDMO), or significant cloud security incidents?
Are updated requirements reflected in cloud service agreements and communicated to cloud service owners?
Is the cloud service inventory reviewed at the same time to capture new services?
suggested_evidence: Cloud cybersecurity requirements review log with dates, reviewers, and outcomes
Version history of cloud cybersecurity requirements document
Records of requirement updates triggered by new cloud service adoption or regulatory changes
Communication records showing updated requirements shared with cloud service owners and procurement
indicators_of_implementation: A defined review schedule exists for cloud cybersecurity requirements
Most recent review falls within the defined cycle
New cloud service adoptions trigger an assessment against requirements before procurement approval

maturity_signals.initial: Cloud requirements never reviewed; requirements are stale as cloud usage evolves
maturity_signals.developing: Occasional reviews with no defined schedule; new cloud services adopted without review
maturity_signals.defined: Periodic review; new cloud services trigger review; updates reflected in requirements and communicated
maturity_signals.advanced: Cloud service governance board reviews requirements quarterly; new cloud service approval gated on requirements review; cloud provider security advisories automatically trigger requirement reassessment

possible_documents: New Cloud Service Approval Process Documentation
possible_technical_evidence: CASB cloud service approval workflow records
Cloud governance board meeting minutes
